project.title: Voices from the Water
project.subtitle: Recovering the Lost Languages of South Florida's First People
project.description: A computational linguistics project using AI to recover the extinct languages of the Calusa, Tequesta, and other indigenous peoples of South Florida. By cross-referencing 16th-century Spanish colonial documents, mission records, and neighboring language families, we are reconstructing words, grammar, and meaning from fragments scattered across five centuries of archives.
project.goal: To give voice back to the people who lived in South Florida for thousands of years before European contact, and ultimately demonstrate a conversation with a native Floridian from the 1500s using an AI-driven Unreal Engine character.

John R. Swanton - Early History of the Creek Indians and Their Neighbors
John H. Hann - Missions to the Calusa
William Marquardt - Calusa and Their Legacy

Victor D. Thompson - Considering Urbanism at Mound Key (Caalus)
Isabelle Holland-Lulewicz - Calusa Fisheries and Estuarine Socio-Ecologies
Ning et al. - Wild Cotton at Mound Key
Stephen E. Reilly - A Marriage of Expedience: The Calusa Indians
Peter Martyr d'Anghiera - De Orbe Novo
Ramón Pané - Relación acerca de las antigüedades de los indios
Herrera - Historia General (Ponce de Leon accounts)

sources.author: Lewis, Smith et al.
sources.relevance: Contains Clifford Lewis's foundational essay on Calusa social organization and religion

sources.name: Bishop Calderon 1675 Letter
sources.author: Calderon, Gabriel Diaz Vara
sources.relevance: Bishop of Cuba's description of Florida missions, native customs, languages

sources.name: Villarreal Letter from Tequesta 1568
sources.author: Francisco de Villarreal
sources.relevance: First letter from Miami. Describes Tequesta customs, witchdoctors, diet, chief's house

sources.name: Worth: 1612 Expedition to Calusa (AGI SD 229)
sources.author: Juan Rodriguez de Cartaya / Worth transcription
sources.relevance: Expedition to Carlos, mentions 60+ Calusa towns, chaguala gold ornaments, Tampa river

sources.name: Worth: 1614 Order re Cacique of Carlos (AGI SD 232)
sources.author: Juan Tribino Guillamas / Worth transcription
sources.relevance: Order to punish Carlos for attacking Mococo; key for understanding Calusa political network

sources.name: Worth: 1630 Testimony re Florida Geography (AGI)
sources.author: Various witnesses / Worth transcription
sources.relevance: Geographic descriptions with indigenous place names and ethnographic detail

sources.name: Worth: 1699 Tampa Bay Expedition Diary (AGI Mexico 618)
sources.author: Diego Pena / Worth transcription
sources.relevance: CRITICAL: 18+ new place names near Tampa Bay, identifies Tampas people, records native terms Araya and Alameava

sources.name: Milanich: Florida's Indians (UFL full text)
sources.author: Jerald Milanich
sources.relevance: Comprehensive overview of all Florida native groups with archaeological and ethnohistorical data

sources.name: Webonary Tunica-English Dictionary
sources.author: Tunica-Biloxi Tribe / Mary Haas
sources.relevance: 3000+ entry Tunica dictionary for testing Granberry's Calusa-Tunica hypothesis

sources.name: Vital Enemies (Santos-Granero)
sources.author: Fernando Santos-Granero
sources.relevance: Chapter on Calusa as capturing society, includes population estimates and political structure

highlights.notes: Native American name for Pensacola Bay, identified as Choctaw origin
Choctaw language form of Ochuse, with apostrophe indicating glottal stop or linguistic notation
Spanish variant spelling of the Choctaw place name
Spanish word meaning lobster, but used here in indigenous context to describe a local shellfish
Spanish gloss provided in parentheses as 'trunk-fish' - likely indigenous term for a type of fish
River name, with Spanish translation 'De las Canoas' (of the canoes) provided in parentheses
This appears to be a Native American place name for an island where deer were found, which seemed unusual to the Spanish
Latin botanical name for a Caribbean tree species, appears to be adopted from indigenous Taíno/Arawakan languages
Spanish word meaning 'turtles' - derived from indigenous observations but hispanicized place name
Derived from indigenous Taíno 'Lukku-cairi' meaning 'island people' - refers to the Bahamas
Indigenous place name in Yucatan
Indigenous river name near Campeche
Likely indigenous-derived place name (modern Campeche)

Indigenous-derived place name

Seminole place name preserving Carlos + hatchie (likely meaning river/water)

Indigenous place name
Tribal/ethnic name, appears to be plural form of Calosa
Indigenous name for region in what became South Carolina

dialogues.title: Ponce de Leon's First Contact (1513)
dialogues.context: Spanish ships anchor off the southwest Florida coast. Calusa warriors paddle out in canoes. The first recorded encounter between Europeans and the Calusa.

dialogues.title: Fontaneda's Captivity (c. 1551)
dialogues.context: A young Spanish boy, Hernando de Escalante Fontaneda, is shipwrecked on the Florida coast and brought before the Calusa king Carlos. He will live among them for 17 years and become the primary source for the Calusa language.

dialogues.title: Menendez Meets King Carlos (1566)
dialogues.context: Pedro Menendez de Aviles, adelantado of La Florida, arrives at the Calusa capital on Mound Key to negotiate with King Carlos. Menendez will marry Carlos's sister Antonia to seal an alliance. Based on accounts by Solis de Meras and Barcia.

dialogues.title: The Mission at Estero Bay (c. 1700)
dialogues.context: A Franciscan friar attempts to convert the Calusa at their settlement near Estero Bay. The Calusa resist, defending their traditional religion centered on the sipi idol. Based on accounts from the Havana missions.

rules: Sentence connective precedes all other elements
Subject before all other elements except connective
Independent object immediately before the verb
Predicative word (verb) at end of clause
Adjective immediately after noun it modifies
Predicate modifiers for location immediately before/after verb

timeline.event: Calusa culture emerges in southwest Florida
Ponce de León makes first European contact with Calusa
Fontaneda shipwrecked, lives among Calusa for 17 years
Menéndez establishes contact with Carlos, Tequesta
Fontaneda writes his Memoir, the primary source of Calusa words
Fr. Alaña's last report on Calusa practices and the idol Sipi
Calusa language goes extinct, remnants flee to Cuba
Gatschet publishes first linguistic analysis of Fontaneda's words
Granberry publishes The Calusa, establishing ~12 glossed words
Zamponi catalogs Calusa as unclassified in comprehensive survey

This project: AI-assisted deep-read of 24 historical texts extracts 9,658 terms. Vision pipeline processes Swanton's comparative PDFs. Corpus expands to thousands of entries across 14+ language families.
